question: Is the swarm's high confirmation rate (97.6%) an artifact of weak testing?
method: Build Popperian degree-of-corroboration scorer (tools/test_severity.py). Score all 679 experiment JSONs on 4 severity dimensions: specificity, riskiness, falsifiability, surprise. Compare severity distributions across verdict types.
expect: Median severity < 0.4. Most CONFIRMED experiments are low-severity (weak tests). FALSIFIED experiments have higher severity than CONFIRMED.
actual: Median severity 0.225. 86.2% of CONFIRMED are weak (severity < 0.35). 0% strong (>= 0.5). FALSIFIED severity 64% higher than CONFIRMED (0.274 vs 0.167).
diff: Prediction confirmed on all 3 axes. Severity even lower than expected (0.225 vs < 0.4). 0% strong confirmed was worse than expected. The asymmetry between FALSIFIED and CONFIRMED severity was the sharpest finding — hard tests produce failures, easy tests produce confirmations.
falsification: Median severity >= 0.4, OR CONFIRMED severity >= FALSIFIED severity, OR >= 20% of CONFIRMED are strong
verdict: CONFIRMED — all 3 predictions validated. The swarm's 97.6% reliability conflates untested claims with genuine corroboration. Hard tests falsify; easy tests confirm.
key_finding: Confirmation bias encoded in test severity — the strongest tests produce falsifications, not confirmations. 86.2% of confirmed experiments would not count as corroborated by Popperian standards.
